title: Recognizing the 159th anniversary of the Civil Rights Act of 1866.
plain_summary: This resolution celebrates the 159th anniversary of the Civil Rights Act of 1866. This law was the first federal law that said all people born in America are citizens and should be treated equally under the law, no matter what race they are. The law was created to stop unfair laws in Southern states after the Civil War ended. Congress passed this important law on April 9, 1866, even though President Johnson tried to stop it.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

sections.title: Resolution Recognizing the 159th Anniversary of the Civil Rights Act of 1866
sections.text: This resolution was introduced in the House of Representatives on April ninth, two thousand twenty five by Representative Jackson of Illinois and ten other representatives. It was sent to the Committee on the Judiciary for review. The resolution recognizes the one hundred fifty ninth anniversary of the Civil Rights Act of eighteen sixty six, which became law on April ninth, eighteen sixty six. This was the first federal law to define who could be a citizen and to say that all citizens must be protected equally by the law. The law was created to fight back against efforts to weaken the Emancipation Proclamation, which had freed enslaved people. After the Civil War, President Andrew Johnson required former Confederate states to end slavery, promise loyalty to the United States, and pay their war debts before they could rejoin the Union. However, Southern states then created new laws designed to restrict freed Black people and force them to work. These laws prevented Black citizens from getting equal protection under the law, owning property, making contracts, and having labor rights. Senator Lyman Trumbull of Illinois introduced the Civil Rights Act in the Senate on January fifth, eighteen sixty six. The Senate voted in favor of the bill thirty three to thirteen on February second, eighteen sixty six. The House of Representatives voted in favor of the bill one hundred eleven to thirty eight on March thirteenth, eighteen sixty six, with thirty four members not voting. President Johnson vetoed the bill on March twenty seventh, eighteen sixty six. The Senate voted thirty three to fifteen to override the president's veto on April sixth, eighteen sixty six. The House also voted to override the veto one hundred twenty two to forty one on April ninth, eighteen sixty six, with twenty one members not voting. Because both chambers of Congress voted to override the president's veto, the Civil Rights Act of eighteen sixty six became law. The House of Representatives now recognizes the importance of the Civil Rights Act of eighteen sixty six and how it established that all Americans, regardless of race, are protected equally under the law. The House also recognizes that this law provided the foundation for the Thirteenth and Fourteenth Amendments to the Constitution. Finally, the House recognizes the importance of the rule of law and supports protecting and defending the rights of all Americans.
